tier2_observations.description: LLM-based factual observations - comparisons with cited sources

findings.ucp_policy: HR001 Whistleblowing Policy

comparison.summary: The two versions appear to be identical in all substantive content, wording, structure, and metadata based on the text provided.
substantive_differences: No substantive differences in process or requirements are detectable from the text provided — both versions share identical Purpose, Scope, Policy Statement, Responsibilities, and Related Documentation sections.

No HE-specific adaptations are visible in the UCP version (e.g., no reference to OfS regulatory requirements, UKVI obligations, academic freedom provisions, or HE-specific governance structures such as a Senate or Academic Board).

Both versions list the same senior postholders (IEG CEO and Principal - Stamford, IEG Deputy CEO and Principal – Peterborough, CFO, Executive Director Business Development, Governance Director), with no UCP-specific role such as a Vice-Chancellor or Academic Registrar named.
scope_differences: Based on the text provided, no scope differences exist between the two versions — both reference 'each college' and 'either college' rather than distinguishing HE from FE contexts.
The UCP version contains no additional HE-specific scope provisions such as coverage of research misconduct, academic integrity, or student complaints distinct from the FE context.
Neither version appears to have been meaningfully differentiated for its stated institutional context based on the text supplied.

findings.ucp_policy: Regulatory Framework B – Academic Regulations Governing Taught Qualifications

comparison.summary: These two documents are not comparable policy equivalents: one is a financial reserves policy for a further education group, the other is an academic regulations framework for a higher education institution.
substantive_differences: IEG FIN010 governs financial reserves management with quantitative thresholds (60% of operating income, 10% net current assets, 60 days cash); BGU Regulatory Framework B governs academic regulations for taught qualifications with no financial metrics.

IEG policy is authored and owned by the Chief Financial Officer and approved by the IEG Corporation; BGU document is authored by the Executive Dean of Teaching, Learning and Student Success and owned by the Vice-Chancellor, authorised by Senate.
IEG policy has a short annual review cycle (issued July 2024, review May 2025); BGU regulations have a three-year review cycle (effective September 2024, next review September 2027).
IEG policy includes explicit named role responsibilities (CEO, CFO, Group Director of Finance, Governing Body); BGU document does not assign individual role responsibilities in the excerpt provided.
IEG policy explicitly permits exceptions for strategic capital expenditure beyond threshold limits; BGU regulations state adherence is mandatory except where discretion is explicitly provided.
IEG policy excludes pension revaluation adjustments under FRS102 from its calculations; BGU document has no financial calculation methodology.

BGU document includes formal equality assessment, legal, information governance, and academic governance assessments, plus trade union and student union consultation requirements; IEG policy has no equivalent consultation or assessment framework documented.
scope_differences: IEG FIN010 covers only financial reserves management including cash levels, restricted/unrestricted funds, and going concern obligations; BGU Framework B covers academic programme regulations, credit frameworks, qualifications, modules, credit transfer, and collaborative provision.

BGU Framework B applies to all taught higher education programmes leading to university qualifications and explicitly extends to collaborative provision; IEG policy applies only to the financial position of the IEG Group entity.

IEG policy references related financial documents (Financial Regulations, Treasury Management Policy, College Financial Handbook); BGU document references academic governance structures and sector best practice alignment.
BGU document includes document control provisions for alternative formats and uncontrolled print copies; IEG policy has no equivalent document control provisions.

IEG policy includes an appendix detailing calculation methodology for operating income and expenditure; BGU document includes appendices governing specific programme types, which is entirely absent from the IEG policy.

findings.ucp_policy: Regulatory Framework B – Academic Regulations Governing Taught Qualifications

comparison.summary: These two documents are not comparable policy equivalents: one is an operational sustainability policy for a further education group, the other is an academic regulatory framework for a higher education institution governing taught qualifications.
substantive_differences: IEG HS029 governs environmental and procurement sustainability practices (energy, waste, travel, recycling); BGU Regulatory Framework B governs academic regulations for taught HE qualifications (modules, credits, awards, programmes).

IEG policy is operational and aspirational in nature, with broad commitments and encouraged behaviours; BGU framework is mandatory and regulatory, with explicit statement that adherence is compulsory except where discretion is explicitly provided.
IEG policy ownership sits with the Chief Operating Officer and Estates/Facilities function; BGU framework is owned by the Vice-Chancellor and authorised by Senate.

IEG policy has a simple two-page flat structure with bullet-point commitments; BGU framework is a multi-chapter versioned regulatory document with formal document administration, table of contents, and appendices.
IEG policy review cycle is approximately 3.5 years (Dec 2021 to July 2025); BGU framework review cycle is 3 years (2024 to 2027) with formal version control and Senate authorisation.

IEG policy applies to both staff and students as participants in sustainability behaviours; BGU framework applies to programmes and qualifications, with students as subjects of academic regulation rather than policy actors.
IEG policy references related internal documents (procurement, carbon reduction, travel); BGU framework references external statutory bodies and sector best practice as its alignment rationale.
scope_differences: IEG HS029 covers: procurement sustainability, energy consumption, waste and recycling, travel planning, IT disposal, and double-sided printing — none of which are addressed in the BGU framework.

BGU Regulatory Framework B covers: module and credit structures, taught qualification awards, programme changes, credit transfer, recognition of prior learning, and collaborative provision — none of which are addressed in the IEG sustainability policy.
BGU framework explicitly covers collaborative and partnership provision with external organisations in an academic context; IEG policy has no equivalent provision.
IEG policy includes specific environmental technologies (solar, wind, ground source heat pumps) as considerations; BGU framework contains no environmental or estates content.

BGU framework includes formal equality assessment, legal, information governance, and academic governance assessments as part of its development process; IEG policy documents no equivalent impact assessments.
IEG policy covers student behavioural expectations (printing, travel); BGU framework covers student academic entitlements and programme conditions.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-SPP001 Higher Education Student Protection Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy governs student conduct and behavioural management across the college group, while the UCP policy is a regulatory compliance document focused on protecting HE students from institutional or course disruption.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is reactive and disciplinary in nature, setting out how misconduct is managed; UCP policy is protective and anticipatory, setting out what students are entitled to if things go wrong institutionally
IEG policy establishes a Code of Conduct and graduated disciplinary procedures (warnings, suspension, exclusion); UCP policy contains no disciplinary procedures at all

IEG policy places responsibilities on staff to enforce and communicate behavioural expectations; UCP policy places obligations on the institution to inform and consult students about changes affecting their studies
IEG policy is driven by internal governance (Policy Approval Group); UCP policy is driven by external regulatory requirement (Office for Students registration condition)

IEG policy includes specific protections and communication requirements for vulnerable groups (under-18s, care-experienced, EHCP holders, EAL students); UCP policy references individual diversity and needs only in general terms

UCP policy details complex awarding body relationships and the implications of those arrangements for students (OU, ARU, Pearson, BGU); IEG policy makes no reference to awarding bodies or academic governance
UCP policy explicitly addresses course and institutional closure risk; IEG policy has no equivalent provision
contradictions: IEG policy states parents/carers of under-18s will always be informed of disciplinary matters, and parents of adults with EHCPs will always be informed; UCP policy, which covers HE students who may include some under-19s, makes no equivalent provision, creating a potential gap for any under-18 HE students

IEG policy applies to all enrolled students including those on college premises or related activities; UCP policy is explicitly scoped to HE students only, meaning HE students at UCP fall under two separate frameworks with no stated hierarchy or reconciliation between them
scope_differences: IEG policy covers apprentices and school students aged 14-16 attending college; UCP policy covers HE students only
IEG policy covers ICT and e-safety behaviour on and off premises; UCP policy does not address conduct or online behaviour
IEG policy covers college transport; UCP policy has no equivalent provision
UCP policy covers sub-contractual partnership delivery sites (e.g. Addict Dance Academy, ESPA); IEG policy does not address provision delivered via third-party partners
UCP policy covers regulatory relationships with multiple awarding bodies and the consequences for students of those arrangements; IEG policy does not address academic or awarding body matters
UCP policy addresses the risk of course or institutional closure and continuity of study obligations; IEG policy has no equivalent provision
IEG policy covers work-related activities and educational visits; UCP policy makes no reference to placement or off-site learning contexts

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-ASS007 Higher Education Student Intermission of Study Procedure

comparison.summary: These are entirely different policies covering unrelated subjects: IEG's policy governs student behaviour and conduct, while UCP's procedure governs temporary breaks from study (intermission); they cannot be meaningfully compared for contradictions.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is a behaviour management framework focused on conduct, discipline, and positive behaviour expectations; UCP procedure is an administrative process for approving and recording temporary suspension of studies.

IEG policy assigns responsibilities to teaching and support staff to actively manage and promote behaviour; UCP procedure assigns responsibilities to staff who handle data collection, storage, and student advice.

IEG policy includes differentiated provisions for specific student groups (under-18s, apprentices, Care Experienced students, students with EHCPs, EAL students); UCP procedure applies uniformly to all HE students with no differentiated cohort provisions visible in the excerpt.
IEG policy has a one-year review cycle (September 2024 to September 2025); UCP procedure has a two-year review cycle (May 2024 to May 2026).

IEG policy includes explicit parental/carer notification rules tied to age and vulnerability; UCP procedure includes no parental notification provisions, consistent with its HE-only adult student context.

UCP procedure explicitly references multiple awarding body regulations (ARU, OU, BGU, Pearson HN) and requires compliance with external academic regulations; IEG policy has no equivalent external regulatory alignment visible.
UCP procedure includes a formal risk analysis section and data protection section referencing GDPR/DPA 2018; IEG policy does not include these sections within the excerpt provided.

UCP procedure references specific associated administrative forms and letters (Intermission Form, success/failure letters); IEG policy references an appendix for procedures but no standalone forms are cited.

IEG policy covers off-site activities, work placements, college transport, and ICT/e-safety behaviour; UCP procedure scope is limited to registered HE students and subcontracted HE provision with no equivalent off-site or online behaviour dimension.
scope_differences: IEG policy covers students aged 14-16 (school partnerships), 16-18, adults, apprentices, and Care Experienced students across further education; UCP procedure covers only Higher Education students registered with UCP.
IEG policy explicitly extends to behaviour on college transport, off-site activities, work placements, and online/ICT conduct; UCP procedure contains no equivalent behavioural or off-campus scope.
UCP procedure explicitly covers subcontracted HE provision; IEG policy makes no reference to subcontracted provision.
UCP procedure addresses funding body evidence requirements and statistical/audit data obligations; IEG policy contains no equivalent funding compliance or audit scope.
IEG policy addresses the role of Virtual Schools and post-16 workers for Care Experienced students; UCP procedure has no equivalent safeguarding or third-party notification scope.
UCP procedure cross-references multiple external awarding body regulatory frameworks; IEG policy operates within an internal college regulatory framework only.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-DIS001 Higher Education Student Disciplinary Policy

comparison.summary: Both policies govern student disciplinary behaviour but IEG's is an FE-wide positive behaviour framework while UCP's is a formal HE-specific disciplinary procedure with distinct legal and regulatory grounding.
substantive_differences: IEG frames the policy around promoting positive behaviour with a restorative, person-centred approach; UCP frames it as a formal disciplinary procedure focused on breach, investigation and outcomes.
UCP explicitly references and aligns with external regulatory frameworks (QAA UK Quality Code, OIA Good Practice Framework); IEG has no equivalent external regulatory anchoring.

UCP explicitly excludes academic misconduct (covered by a separate Academic Integrity Policy) and serious misconduct such as harassment and sexual misconduct (covered by a separate Unacceptable Behaviour Policy); IEG makes no such explicit policy demarcation.
UCP includes a specific enumerated list of disciplinary offences (fraud, drug/alcohol misuse, obstruction, misuse of social media etc.); IEG does not enumerate specific offences in the visible text.

IEG includes explicit provisions for specific vulnerable groups: Care Experienced students, students with EHCPs or high-needs funding, students with English as a second language, and students aged under 18; UCP makes no equivalent distinctions in the visible text.
IEG explicitly covers apprentices and their sponsoring employers as a distinct category; UCP has no equivalent provision.
IEG places active behavioural responsibilities on all staff with specific consistency requirements; UCP's visible text does not assign equivalent staff-facing duties in the same prescriptive way.
IEG covers behaviour on college transport as an explicit jurisdictional area; UCP does not mention transport.
UCP explicitly states the policy cannot replace civil or criminal processes and acknowledges that parallel processes may be more appropriate; IEG makes no such statement.
UCP applies to sub-contractual provision; IEG covers students from partner schools (aged 14-16) attending college, reflecting FE's broader age range and delivery model.
contradictions: IEG states parents/carers of students with EHCPs or high-needs funding 'will always be informed' of disciplinary action regardless of age; UCP contains no equivalent provision and does not address parental notification at all, meaning HE students with equivalent needs at UCP would have no such protection under this policy.

IEG's scope includes behaviour off-premises where it relates to ICT/e-safety; UCP's off-premises scope is tied to reputational harm to UCP — these are materially different jurisdictional triggers for the same category of behaviour (e.g. a student posting harmful content online could be in scope under both policies but for different stated reasons).
scope_differences: IEG covers students aged 14-16 from partner schools; UCP is HE-only and has no equivalent provision.
IEG explicitly covers apprentices and employer notification; UCP has no apprenticeship scope.
IEG covers college transport; UCP does not.
UCP covers sub-contractual HE provision; IEG has no equivalent sub-contractual clause in the visible text.

UCP explicitly excludes academic misconduct and serious misconduct (harassment, sexual misconduct) by signposting to separate policies; IEG does not make these exclusions explicit in the visible text.
UCP explicitly addresses the relationship between institutional disciplinary procedures and civil or criminal processes; IEG does not.
IEG includes specific vulnerable learner categories (Care Experienced, EHCP, EAL, under-18) with tailored procedural obligations; UCP does not address these groups in the visible text.

UCP includes an explicit list of qualifying disciplinary conduct categories including drug and alcohol possession on premises, fraud, and obstruction of staff; IEG does not enumerate equivalent categories in the visible text.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-COM001 Higher Education Student Complaints Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy governs student behaviour and conduct for the broader college group, while the UCP policy governs HE-specific student complaints resolution with a formal multi-stage process.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is preventative and disciplinary in nature (managing and shaping behaviour); UCP policy is reactive and remedial (resolving dissatisfaction after the fact)

UCP policy has a defined 3-stage formal escalation process (informal resolution, formal complaint, review/appeal); IEG policy has no equivalent staged escalation structure visible in the provided text
UCP policy references external regulatory bodies (OIA, OfS, CMA, SPA) and awarding bodies (OU, Pearson, BGU) as part of its framework; IEG policy has no external regulatory references
UCP policy requires exhaustion of informal routes before formal complaint can be made; IEG policy has no equivalent sequencing requirement visible in the provided text
UCP policy uses named forms (ICF, CS2) and a specific digital channel (CANVAS, student handbook) for communication; IEG policy does not reference equivalent formal documentation tools

IEG policy includes specific provisions for vulnerable groups (under-18s, care-experienced students, EHCP holders, EAL students, apprentices); UCP policy does not include equivalent group-specific provisions in the provided text
IEG policy explicitly addresses off-site conduct, college transport, and ICT/e-safety behaviour; UCP policy scope is limited to course and service provision
IEG policy assigns responsibilities to all college staff for embedding and enforcing standards; UCP policy assigns procedural responsibilities to the Faculty HE Manager and Academic Director
contradictions: UCP policy states it covers 'all prescribed higher education provision operating through UCP' as the HE arm of IEG, which could create ambiguity about which policy applies to HE students on IEG premises who also exhibit conduct issues — the IEG behaviour policy claims to cover all enrolled students, potentially including UCP HE students, while UCP has its own separate complaints framework

IEG policy states parents/carers of students with EHCPs or high-needs funding 'will always be informed' of disciplinary action; UCP as an HE provider would typically be dealing with adults where such automatic parental notification would conflict with data protection and adult confidentiality norms — UCP policy makes no provision for this and implies confidential handling as standard
scope_differences: IEG policy covers school students aged 14-16 attending college, apprentices, and students on transport; UCP policy covers none of these groups
IEG policy explicitly covers conduct on college transport and off-site educational visits; UCP policy does not
IEG policy covers ICT and e-safety behaviour on or off premises; UCP policy does not address this
UCP policy covers applicants (pre-enrolment) as well as enrolled students; IEG policy applies only to enrolled students
UCP policy covers franchised partner provision and multiple campuses (Peterborough and Stamford); IEG policy does not explicitly reference franchised provision
UCP policy covers complaints about fellow students, employees, services, and departments; IEG policy governs student conduct only, not complaints against the institution
IEG policy includes provisions specific to care-experienced students and virtual school notification; UCP policy makes no equivalent provision
UCP policy references awarding body involvement (OU, Pearson, BGU) at the review stage; IEG policy has no equivalent external body involvement

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-ATT001 Higher Education Student Attendance Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy governs student behaviour and conduct across the whole college group, while the UCP policy specifically governs HE student attendance and engagement at a university centre level; they address adjacent but largely distinct regulatory concerns.
substantive_differences: IEG policy covers the full spectrum of behaviour management including misconduct, disciplinary procedures, sanctions, and positive behaviour promotion; UCP policy is narrowly focused on attendance monitoring, recording, and reporting obligations.

IEG policy includes explicit escalating disciplinary procedures (warnings, suspension, exclusion); UCP policy does not describe disciplinary sanctions but focuses on monitoring triggers and reporting to third parties.
UCP policy has a specific 15-minute lateness rule after which a tutor may refuse entry and record the student as absent; IEG policy has no equivalent time-based attendance threshold.

UCP policy explicitly references reporting duties to external bodies such as Student Finance England, sponsors, and employers as a named procedural purpose; IEG policy references employer notification only in the context of apprentices.
UCP policy mandates compulsory attendance at enrolment and induction sessions as a distinct requirement; IEG policy does not specify induction attendance as a separate compulsory obligation.

IEG policy includes specific safeguarding-linked provisions: under-18 parental notification, Care Experienced student notifications to Virtual School and post-16 worker, EHCP/high-needs adult provisions, and EAL support; UCP policy contains none of these.
IEG policy explicitly addresses ICT and e-safety behaviour on and off premises and on college transport; UCP policy does not address these areas.
UCP policy applies to sub-contractual and partner provision; IEG policy does not mention sub-contracted or partner delivery arrangements.
IEG policy is reviewed annually (September 2024 to September 2025); UCP policy has an approximately 18-month review cycle (February 2026 to July 2027).
IEG policy is approved by a Policy Approval Group; UCP policy is approved by Academic Board, reflecting the HE governance structure.
contradictions: IEG policy frames parental contact for under-18s as a standard expectation with opt-out possible at age 18; UCP policy makes no reference to parental contact, which is consistent with HE norms but creates a potential gap if any UCP students are under 18 at enrolment and no bridging provision is documented.

IEG policy places responsibility for managing low-level disruption on all staff in all college areas; UCP policy gives tutors discretion to refuse entry to late students, implying a different locus of control over classroom disruption management that is not reconciled with the IEG-wide staff responsibilities described in the IEG policy.
scope_differences: IEG policy covers all enrolled students across all levels (14-16 school students, 16-18, adults, apprentices, HE); UCP policy applies exclusively to Higher Education provision.
IEG policy covers behaviour on college transport and during educational visits; UCP policy does not address off-site or transport contexts.
IEG policy addresses apprentice-specific conduct and employer notification obligations; UCP policy does not cover apprentices.
UCP policy explicitly covers sub-contractual and partner HE provision including UCP at Stamford; IEG policy contains no equivalent partner/sub-contract scope.

UCP policy references a suite of related HE-specific documents (Intermission of Study, Fitness to Practise, Fitness to Study); IEG policy references its own internal appendices but not equivalent HE regulatory documents.
IEG policy covers ICT use and e-safety as behavioural matters; UCP policy does not.
UCP policy includes data/audit and statistical reporting as an explicit procedural purpose; IEG policy does not address data reporting obligations.
IEG policy covers safeguarding-linked groups (Care Experienced, EHCP, EAL, under-18s) with named procedures; UCP policy contains no equivalent group-specific provisions within this document.

findings.ucp_policy: Code of Practice for Applying for Student Ethical Approval at University Centre Peterborough

comparison.summary: These two documents serve entirely different regulatory purposes: IEG's policy governs student behavioural conduct across a mixed-age FE college group, while UCP's Code of Practice governs the ethical approval process for student-led academic research at a higher education institution.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is disciplinary/behavioural in nature, defining misconduct, sanctions, and staff responsibilities; UCP document is a procedural compliance framework for research ethics approval with no disciplinary content
IEG operates a staff-enforced top-down behaviour management model; UCP places primary compliance responsibility on the student researcher themselves
IEG includes age-differentiated provisions (under-18, 14-16 school students) and parental notification rules; UCP has no age-differentiated provisions, implying an adult HE-only population
UCP uses a tiered risk-category system (Green, Yellow, Blue, Red) to determine the approval pathway; IEG has no equivalent risk-categorisation framework
UCP requires formal application, committee review, documented consent, and Participant Information Sheets; IEG requires no formal application process, only staff-managed interventions
UCP references extensive external legislation (GDPR, Mental Capacity Act, Terrorism Act, Medical Devices Regulations, etc.); IEG references no specific legislation beyond implicit safeguarding duties
UCP includes provisions for NHS/Social Care approvals, DBS checks, Ministry of Defence research, and international research; IEG has no equivalent external body coordination requirements
IEG explicitly covers online/ICT behaviour and e-safety; UCP covers internet-based research methodology but not general online conduct
UCP mandates ethics training for students, supervisors, and committee members as a formal requirement; IEG has no equivalent mandatory training component specified within the policy

IEG includes specific provisions for Care Experienced students, EHCP holders, apprentices, and EAL students; UCP makes no equivalent protected characteristic or vulnerability-based distinctions in its procedures
scope_differences: IEG covers conduct on college premises, off-site activities, college transport, and work placements; UCP does not address physical premises conduct at all
UCP covers research taking place outside the UK, involving animals, and within the criminal justice system; IEG has no equivalent research-activity scope
IEG covers employer notification for apprentices; UCP has no employer or sponsor notification provisions
UCP covers intellectual property, insurance, equipment checks, and data storage requirements; IEG covers none of these
IEG covers parental/carer involvement and virtual school notification for looked-after children; UCP has no parental involvement provisions
UCP covers collaborative research, reuse of previous data, pilot studies, and conflicts of interest in a research context; IEG has no research governance provisions
IEG addresses low-level classroom disruption and in-lesson behaviour management; UCP has no classroom management dimension
UCP covers block ethical approval for multiple similar studies; IEG has no equivalent batch or grouped approval mechanism
IEG explicitly addresses the college's duty to provide a safe environment for staff and visitors as well as students; UCP is student-researcher focused and does not address staff or visitor safety

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-COM002 Higher Education Visitors, Customers and Stakeholders Complaints Policy

comparison.summary: IEG CQ006 is a broad group-wide complaints policy covering FE students, apprentices, and employers, while UCP-COM002 is a narrower HE-specific policy covering only external visitors, customers, and stakeholders (explicitly excluding current students and staff).
substantive_differences: IEG CQ006 covers compliments as well as complaints; UCP-COM002 covers complaints and dissatisfaction only, with no formal compliments process

IEG CQ006 includes a named role structure (Quality Officer, Investigating Officer, Assistant Principal Quality) with defined responsibilities; UCP-COM002 does not specify named internal roles in the extracted text

UCP-COM002 explicitly lists eight specific categories of complaint that fall within scope (e.g. freedom of speech, external speaker conduct, administrative procedure failures); IEG CQ006 does not enumerate complaint categories
UCP-COM002 explicitly addresses freedom of speech and external speaker complaints as a distinct category, referencing FREE001; IEG CQ006 has no such provision
IEG CQ006 includes a safeguarding escalation duty (reporting to Local Authority); UCP-COM002 contains no equivalent safeguarding provision in the extracted text
IEG CQ006 states complaints received anonymously may not be accepted; UCP-COM002 makes no statement on anonymous complaints
IEG CQ006 explicitly states complaints are communicated to students during Induction and reviewed annually by Group Governors; UCP-COM002 contains no equivalent governance or induction requirements
UCP-COM002 explicitly encourages informal resolution as a principle; IEG CQ006 does not state this as a guiding principle
UCP-COM002 includes an explicit privacy and data disclosure principle; IEG CQ006 does not
IEG CQ006 applies to apprentices and employers as a named group; UCP-COM002 does not cover apprentices or employers
UCP-COM002 references a separate companion policy (COM001) for current HE students; IEG CQ006 references UCP's separate policy only as an exception carve-out
contradictions: IEG CQ006 states its scope excludes students on courses at University Centre Peterborough (directing them to a separate policy), while UCP-COM002 also excludes current UCP students (directing them to COM001) — these are consistent in outcome but create a potential gap: it is not explicitly confirmed in either document that COM001 is the policy IEG CQ006 is deferring to, risking ambiguity about which policy governs current UCP students

IEG CQ006 implies staff complaints that constitute grievances are redirected to the Grievance Procedure, referencing no specific policy code; UCP-COM002 directs staff to IEG HR013 Staff Harassment and Bullying Policy specifically — these are not the same policy, meaning a UCP staff member reading IEG CQ006 could be directed to a different process than one reading UCP-COM002
scope_differences: IEG CQ006 covers FE students across all IEG sites (Peterborough, Stamford, Inspire Insights); UCP-COM002 covers only UCP and does not apply to other IEG sites
IEG CQ006 covers apprentices and their employers; UCP-COM002 does not cover apprentices or employers
UCP-COM002 covers external visitors, prospective customers, and stakeholders who are not students or staff; IEG CQ006 does not explicitly address this non-student, non-staff external audience
UCP-COM002 covers complaints about freedom of speech and external speaker conduct; IEG CQ006 has no equivalent coverage
UCP-COM002 covers complaints about UCP's failure to follow administrative procedures as a named category; IEG CQ006 does not enumerate this as a distinct category
IEG CQ006 covers compliments and positive feedback as a formal process; UCP-COM002 does not
IEG CQ006 includes a safeguarding escalation pathway; UCP-COM002 does not address safeguarding
UCP-COM002 explicitly addresses the scenario where a complaint is about a third-party organisation unrelated to UCP, advising redirection; IEG CQ006 has no equivalent provision

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-COM001 Higher Education Student Complaints Policy

comparison.summary: The UCP HE policy is a detailed, HE-specific complaints procedure that supersedes the IEG Group policy for UCP students, with significantly more stages, external regulatory alignment, and formal governance.
substantive_differences: UCP policy has a defined three-stage process (Early Resolution, Formal Complaint, Review Stage); IEG policy does not specify numbered stages in the policy document itself, referencing procedures in an Appendix 2 not provided
UCP policy explicitly references and aligns with external regulatory bodies: OIA, OfS Designated Quality Body, CMA, and SPA; IEG policy has no external regulatory references
UCP policy is approved by the Academic Board; IEG policy is approved by a Policy Approval Group and reviewed by Group Governors
UCP policy includes a Stage 3 independent review route via awarding bodies (OU, Pearson, BGU); IEG policy has no equivalent external escalation route
UCP policy requires exhaustion of informal routes before formal complaint is accepted; IEG policy does not explicitly state this requirement
UCP policy specifies use of named forms (Informal Complaint Form/ICF and CS2 form); IEG policy references a generic complaints form on the website or student hub
UCP policy explicitly covers applicants as well as students; IEG policy does not explicitly include applicants
UCP policy states complaints are handled confidentially; IEG policy does not use the term confidentiality
UCP policy covers franchised partners; IEG policy does not mention franchised provision
UCP policy includes complaints about treatment by fellow students; IEG policy does not explicitly include peer-on-peer complaints
IEG policy explicitly covers compliments and has mechanisms to record them; UCP policy title and content is complaints-only with no compliments process
IEG policy explicitly addresses Apprentice and Employer complaints; UCP policy does not
IEG policy references a safeguarding duty to report to Local Authority within the complaints policy; UCP policy does not include this provision within the complaints document

IEG policy names specific responsible roles (Quality Officer, Investigating Officer, Assistant Principal Quality); UCP policy names the Faculty HE Manager for Stage 1 but roles for later stages are not detailed in the extracted text
contradictions: The IEG policy states its scope excludes UCP students ('The only exception is where a student is on a course of study at University Centre Peterborough which has a separate policy'), which is consistent with the UCP policy existing, but the IEG policy still lists UCP as a site in its footer, creating potential ambiguity about administrative scope
IEG policy states anonymous complaints 'may not be accepted'; UCP policy does not include this restriction, meaning UCP students have no explicit bar on anonymous complaints while IEG students do

IEG policy states complaints are communicated to all students during Induction; UCP policy states clear lines of communication are published on CANVAS and in the student handbook, representing different communication mechanisms for essentially the same population site
scope_differences: IEG policy covers FE students, apprentices, and employers across IEG group; UCP policy covers only HE students and applicants
UCP policy covers franchised partner provision; IEG policy does not
UCP policy covers complaints about fellow students; IEG policy does not explicitly include this
UCP policy covers applicants (pre-enrolment); IEG policy does not explicitly include applicants
IEG policy includes compliments recording and management; UCP policy does not
IEG policy includes a staff grievance crossover clause; UCP policy does not address this
UCP policy covers multiple awarding body relationships (OU, Pearson, BGU) with escalation routes to those bodies; IEG policy has no equivalent

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-ASS005 Higher Education Academic Appeal Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy is a shorter, FE-focused framework covering all IEG qualifications except UCP, while the UCP policy is a detailed HE-specific document governed by HE regulatory bodies and with significantly more procedural granularity.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is governed by JCQ and Awarding Body regulations; UCP policy is governed by OIA, QAA, CMA, and SPA guidance, reflecting different regulatory regimes for FE vs HE
UCP policy explicitly defines 'Academic Appeal' using the OIA definition; IEG policy has no formal definition section

UCP policy lists specific named academic bodies that can make appealable decisions (Exam Board, Student Review Subcommittee, Academic Integrity Panel, Extenuating Circumstance Panel); IEG policy has no equivalent body structure
UCP policy specifies a 15 working day deadline from formal publication of results for former students to submit appeals; IEG policy states no explicit time limit for submission
UCP policy explicitly prohibits 'twin tracking' (pursuing an appeal and complaint simultaneously); IEG policy has no such prohibition
UCP policy distinguishes appeals from admissions appeals and complaints, directing each to separate policies; IEG policy makes no such distinctions
UCP policy covers franchise partners (ESPA and Addict) and multiple campuses (Peterborough College, Stamford College); IEG policy does not reference franchise arrangements
IEG policy assigns named responsibility to the Assistant Principal Quality as the primary owner of the process; UCP policy attributes origination to the UCP Academic Office with approval via ASQC
IEG policy states students must be informed at induction and that the policy must be referenced in all Google Classrooms; UCP policy has no equivalent digital platform communication requirement
IEG policy explicitly provides for a student to be accompanied at a hearing by a student, relative, or employer representative; UCP policy (in the provided excerpt) does not specify this right
UCP policy specifies that disagreement with academic judgement alone cannot constitute grounds for appeal; IEG policy does not make this distinction explicitly
UCP policy review cycle is approximately annual (Aug 2024, review July 2025); IEG policy has a two-year review cycle (Dec 2024, review Dec 2026)
contradictions: IEG policy states it applies to 'all students and qualifications delivered by IEG, except those provided by University Centre Peterborough'; UCP policy states it covers 'all higher education provision operating through UCP which includes Peterborough College and Stamford College' — this means students at Peterborough College and Stamford College studying HE programmes fall under UCP policy, but it is ambiguous whether FE students at those same campuses are clearly covered by IEG policy, creating a potential coverage gap or overlap at shared sites
scope_differences: UCP policy covers micro-credentials explicitly; IEG policy does not mention micro-credentials
UCP policy covers former students (within 15 working days of results); IEG policy scope is limited to current students with no reference to former students
UCP policy covers appeals related to academic misconduct decisions, reasonable adjustments for disabilities, thesis submission, and progression rules; IEG policy does not address these categories
UCP policy covers franchise partner institutions (ESPA, Addict); IEG policy has no franchise scope

IEG policy explicitly covers the right to appeal against a centre's decision to reject internally assessed work on grounds of student malpractice; UCP policy (in the provided excerpt) does not explicitly list this as a ground

IEG policy includes obligations around plagiarism policy awareness and Internal Quality Assurance procedures as part of the appeals governance framework; UCP policy treats academic integrity as a separate panel/process
UCP policy references a separate admissions appeals route (UCP-REC001) and a separate complaints route (UCP-COM001); IEG policy does not signpost to equivalent separate policies for those scenarios

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-ASS004 Higher Education Academic Integrity Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy governs academic appeals for non-HE qualifications across the group, while the UCP policy governs academic integrity (misconduct) specifically for HE provision, making them complementary rather than directly equivalent documents.
substantive_differences: The IEG document is an Appeals Policy (challenging marks/grades); the UCP document is an Academic Integrity Policy (handling misconduct) — they address different subject matter despite both falling under academic quality.

IEG explicitly references JCQ and Awarding Body regulations as its compliance framework; UCP references multiple awarding bodies (Open University, Bishop Grosseteste University, Pearson HN) with HE-specific regulatory frameworks.

IEG assigns named responsibility to the Assistant Principal Quality for operational oversight; UCP assigns overall responsibility to the Academic Director with delegated day-to-day responsibility to identified staff.
IEG includes a specific entitlement for students to be accompanied at appeal hearings (by a student, relative, or employer representative); no equivalent right is visible in the UCP excerpt.
UCP includes a formal Risk Analysis section and a Data Protection section with GDPR/DPA 2018 compliance details; the IEG policy contains neither of these sections.
UCP mandates annual staff training delivered by the Student Support Team; IEG policy places no equivalent recurring training obligation on staff.

IEG specifies that the appeals policy must be referenced in all Google Classrooms and communicated at induction; UCP makes no equivalent channel-specific communication requirement visible in the excerpt.
IEG outlines a sequential internal review process before escalation to the Awarding Body; UCP's procedural escalation steps are not visible in the provided excerpt.
UCP explicitly covers subcontracted provision within its scope; IEG policy scope language does not address subcontracted delivery.
UCP explicitly extends scope to ethical misconduct linked to research and Undergraduate Major Projects/Dissertations; IEG has no equivalent research-specific provision.
contradictions: No direct contradictions are present; the IEG policy explicitly excludes UCP provision from its scope ('except those provided by University Centre Peterborough, for which a separate policy is in place'), and the UCP policy states it applies exclusively to HE provision — the two policies are intentionally non-overlapping.

The UCP document references a separate UCP-ASS005 Higher Education Academic Appeal Policy for appeals, meaning the UCP equivalent of the IEG Appeals Policy is a different document entirely from the one provided here — the comparison is therefore between non-equivalent document types.
scope_differences: IEG policy covers all qualifications delivered by the IEG Group (FE, vocational, etc.) but explicitly excludes UCP HE provision; UCP policy covers only HE provision.

UCP policy covers academic misconduct/integrity (plagiarism, malpractice, ethical breaches); IEG policy covers the right to appeal assessment outcomes — these are distinct processes with no overlap in subject matter.
UCP scope includes subcontracted HE provision; IEG scope does not address subcontracting.
UCP explicitly includes research ethics and dissertation/major project misconduct; IEG policy contains no research-specific scope.

IEG policy addresses the student's right to challenge a centre's rejection of work on malpractice grounds; UCP policy addresses the institution's handling of student malpractice — opposite perspectives on the same issue.
UCP includes data protection obligations and GDPR compliance as an in-scope policy requirement; IEG policy does not address data protection within its scope.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-FREE001 Higher Education Academic Freedom of Speech and External Speaker Policy

comparison.summary: These are two entirely different policies covering different subject matter: IEG's covers academic appeals against assessment grades, while UCP's covers academic freedom of speech and external speakers.
substantive_differences: IEG policy governs the process for students appealing assessment marks, grades, or outcomes; UCP policy governs freedom of speech, academic freedom of enquiry, and the organisation of events and external speakers.

IEG policy is procedural and operational, detailing a step-by-step internal review and escalation process to Awarding Bodies (JCQ-aligned); UCP policy is rights-based and legislative, grounded in the Education (No 2) Act 1986 s.43, Counter-Terrorism and Security Act 2015, Equality Act 2010, and Human Rights Act 1998.

IEG policy assigns responsibility to the Assistant Principal Quality for managing reviews, instructing reviewers, and escalating to Awarding Bodies; UCP policy assigns origination to the UCP Academic Director and approval to the HE Academic Board.

IEG policy requires appeals to be submitted in writing with a clear deadline; UCP policy requires procedural compliance for event organisation and speaker vetting with no equivalent submission deadline mechanism.

IEG policy includes a companion right (to be accompanied at hearings by a student, relative, or employer representative); UCP policy includes no equivalent companion right but does include conduct requirements for attendees.
IEG policy is reviewed every two years (next: December 2026); UCP policy has a review date of July 2027 and is already at Issue 3 (May 2025).
UCP policy explicitly balances freedom of speech against Prevent duties and counter-terrorism legislation; IEG policy has no equivalent security or counter-terrorism dimension.
scope_differences: IEG policy explicitly excludes UCP-provided qualifications from its scope; UCP policy applies specifically to the HE context and covers persons IEG policy does not, such as external speakers, visiting lecturers, premises hirers, and Student Union members.

IEG policy covers only students enrolled on qualifications delivered by IEG (non-HE); UCP policy covers students on intermission, HE Council members, and sub-committee members, none of whom are addressed by the IEG policy.
UCP policy covers off-site and online UCP-branded events; IEG policy has no equivalent off-site or online event dimension.
IEG policy covers malpractice by students as a ground for appeal rejection; UCP policy does not address malpractice.
UCP policy includes an Equality Impact Assessment requirement; IEG policy does not reference an equivalent assessment.
IEG policy covers the integrity of qualifications and interaction with external Awarding Bodies; UCP policy does not address qualification integrity or Awarding Body relationships.

findings.ucp_policy: Regulatory Framework B – Academic Regulations Governing Taught Qualifications

comparison.summary: The IEG policy governs FE-level appeals under JCQ/Awarding Body frameworks, while the BGU/UCP document is a comprehensive HE regulatory framework governing taught qualifications under university Senate authority, of which appeals would be one component.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is solely focused on academic appeals; the BGU document is a broad regulatory framework covering modules, credits, qualifications, programmes, and more — appeals are only one potential section within it
IEG appeals are governed by JCQ and Awarding Body regulations; BGU operates under its own Senate-authorised regulations aligned to HE sector best practice

IEG assigns responsibility for appeals oversight to an Assistant Principal Quality; BGU's framework is owned by the Vice-Chancellor and authored by the Executive Dean of Teaching, Learning and Student Success
IEG explicitly provides students the right to be accompanied at appeal hearings by a student, relative, or employer representative; no equivalent right is stated in the BGU excerpt provided

IEG includes a specific provision for escalation to the Awarding Body when internal processes are exhausted; BGU as a degree-awarding body is itself the final internal authority, with escalation typically going to the Office of the Independent Adjudicator (OIA) — though this is not visible in the excerpt
IEG policy has a two-year review cycle (Dec 2024–Dec 2026); BGU framework reviews every three years (next due September 2027)
IEG requires the appeals policy to be referenced in all Google Classrooms and communicated at induction; BGU has no equivalent dissemination requirement visible in the excerpt

BGU framework underwent equality assessment, legal review, information governance review, and consultation with trade unions and the Students' Union; IEG policy shows no equivalent formal consultation or assessment process
BGU regulations are mandatory except where explicit discretion is provided; IEG policy language is less prescriptive in tone
contradictions: No direct contradictions are present; the two documents explicitly acknowledge separation of scope — IEG's policy states it does not apply to UCP, and UCP/BGU operates under an entirely separate regulatory authority
scope_differences: IEG policy covers only academic appeals; BGU document covers the full regulatory framework for taught qualifications including credit, modules, programme changes, credit transfer, and more
IEG policy applies to all IEG qualifications excluding UCP; BGU regulations apply specifically to HE taught qualifications classified by Senate, including collaborative provision
BGU framework explicitly addresses collaborative provision and programme-specific appendices; IEG policy has no equivalent provision
IEG policy addresses malpractice-related appeals (e.g., rejected internally assessed work); the BGU excerpt does not visibly address this in the portion provided
BGU framework specifies applicability dates per programme type (e.g., Foundation Year vs. other awards); IEG policy has a single uniform effective date
IEG policy explicitly references plagiarism policy awareness as a responsibility; no equivalent cross-reference is visible in the BGU excerpt
BGU document includes formal document administration metadata including alternative format access instructions; IEG policy has no accessibility provision stated

findings.ucp_policy: UCP- FIT002 Higher Education Fitness to Study Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy is a high-level framework covering all students with a staged intervention model, while the UCP policy excerpt covers only the formal appeals procedure before the UCP Council, making them complementary but operating at different procedural depths.
substantive_differences: The IEG policy describes a three-stage tiered intervention model based on perceived risk level; the UCP document contains no equivalent staged intervention model in the excerpt provided.
The IEG policy explicitly allows invocation before admission, including refusal of a place during induction if needs cannot be met; no equivalent pre-admission provision appears in the UCP excerpt.
The IEG policy links non-engagement to potential disciplinary action or withdrawal; the UCP excerpt addresses only the appeals route after a committee decision, not consequences for non-engagement.

The UCP appeals procedure specifies precise working-day deadlines (10 days to appeal, 5 days notice to student representative, 7 days for statements, 15 days written notice before hearing); the IEG policy specifies no equivalent procedural timelines.

The UCP procedure grants the student the right to be accompanied by a friend or elected Student Representative at the hearing, with explicit rules about witnesses; the IEG policy contains no equivalent hearing representation rights in the provided text.
The UCP procedure requires written notice by hand or recorded/special delivery to the student's last known address; the IEG policy specifies no equivalent formal notification method.

The UCP procedure includes an elected Student Representative involvement option and requires the student's consent regarding their presence; the IEG policy makes no reference to student representative involvement in proceedings.

The UCP procedure establishes a formal committee structure with a Chair chosen from members and a record-keeping secretary; the IEG policy does not describe equivalent committee governance in the provided text.
The UCP procedure defines a specific order of evidence presentation (student first, then institution); the IEG policy contains no equivalent procedural sequence.

The IEG policy explicitly references early intervention to avoid crisis and frames the policy around wellbeing support; the UCP excerpt is solely procedural and contains no equivalent wellbeing framing.
contradictions: The IEG policy states UCP students are explicitly excluded from its scope ('The only exception is where a student is on a course of study at the University Centre Peterborough, which has a separate policy'), confirming the two policies are intended to apply to mutually exclusive populations — meaning any procedural inconsistency between them is by design rather than accidental contradiction, but it does confirm they must not be applied concurrently to UCP students.

No direct procedural contradictions exist between the two documents as provided, because the IEG text covers early-stage intervention and the UCP text covers only the end-stage appeals process; they do not address the same procedural moment.
scope_differences: The IEG policy covers all students and apprentices across the group except UCP HE students; the UCP policy applies exclusively to UCP Higher Education students.
The IEG policy addresses the full lifecycle from pre-admission concerns through to withdrawal; the UCP excerpt covers only the formal appeals stage after a committee decision.
The IEG policy explicitly includes apprentices within its scope; the UCP policy makes no reference to apprentices.
The UCP policy references a Fitness to Practise Committee as a distinct body alongside the Fitness to Study Committee, suggesting a professional/vocational dimension not present in the IEG policy.
The UCP policy references payment failure as a separate appeals route to the Academic Director, a financial dimension entirely absent from the IEG policy.
The IEG policy addresses risk to others and campus safety as triggers for the policy; the UCP excerpt does not address triggers, only post-decision appeals.
The UCP policy includes a Student Discipline Committee within the same appeals framework, suggesting integration with a broader disciplinary structure not visible in the IEG policy excerpt.
The IEG policy references reasonable adjustments under the Equality Act 2010; no equivalent equalities framing appears in the UCP excerpt.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP- FIT001 Higher Education Fitness to Practise Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy addresses student health and wellbeing fitness to study for FE/apprentices, while the UCP policy addresses professional suitability and public protection for HE students on professionally accredited programmes.
substantive_differences: IEG policy is triggered by health, wellbeing or risk concerns affecting ability to study; UCP policy is triggered by failure to meet professional standards and public protection concerns
IEG policy has three formal escalating stages based on perceived risk level; no equivalent staged structure is described in the UCP policy excerpt
UCP policy explicitly covers criminal convictions and DBS check failures as grounds for action; IEG policy does not mention criminal convictions or DBS
UCP policy includes conduct on placement settings external to campus as within scope; IEG policy refers only to campus-based and study-related concerns
UCP policy is framed around protecting the public and professional registration eligibility (PSRB requirements); IEG policy is framed around supporting the student's learning and academic achievement
UCP policy can action behaviour in non-UCP settings if it compromises professional standards; IEG policy does not extend scope beyond study and campus contexts
IEG policy explicitly applies to apprentices; UCP policy applies only to HE provision including subcontractual arrangements

IEG policy allows refusal of a place at application or during induction; UCP policy references unsuitability for enrolment but frames it around professional programme entry rather than general admission
contradictions: IEG policy states UCP students are explicitly excluded from its scope ('The only exception is where a student is on a course of study at the University Centre Peterborough, which has a separate policy'), confirming the two policies are intended to be mutually exclusive in application, meaning simultaneous application to the same student would be contradictory by design

IEG policy treats illness primarily as a health and wellbeing support matter; UCP policy lists illness as a ground for fitness to practise concern alongside criminal convictions and misconduct, reflecting a materially different framing of the same trigger
scope_differences: IEG policy covers FE students and apprentices; UCP policy covers HE students only (including subcontracted HE provision)
UCP policy is limited to professionally accredited programmes requiring PSRB registration; IEG policy applies to all programmes regardless of professional accreditation
UCP policy covers behaviour at placement settings; IEG policy does not address placements
UCP policy covers undisclosed prior information and criminal history; IEG policy does not address these
UCP policy covers conduct standards specific to professions involving children, young people and vulnerable adults; IEG policy has no profession-specific conduct standards
IEG policy explicitly addresses the most vulnerable students as a named group requiring support; UCP policy does not single out vulnerable students in the same way

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-FIT002 Higher Education Fitness to Study Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG Group policy is a shorter, high-level framework covering all students (FE, apprentices, and HE), while the UCP policy is a longer, HE-specific document that explicitly supersedes the IEG policy for UCP students and provides more detailed procedural guidance.
substantive_differences: The IEG policy has three formal stages based on perceived risk level; the UCP policy does not reference a three-stage structure in the excerpted text and instead emphasises a supportive vs. disciplinary decision pathway.

The IEG policy explicitly contemplates pre-admission refusal of a place and withdrawal during induction if needs cannot be met; the UCP policy focuses on students already enrolled and their continuation or return to study.

The UCP policy explicitly frames the procedure as an alternative to disciplinary action ('supportive rather than disciplinary approach wherever possible'), whereas the IEG policy lists disciplinary procedures and withdrawal as direct consequences of failure to engage.
The UCP policy references sub-contractual provision within its scope; the IEG policy makes no mention of sub-contractual arrangements.
The UCP policy cross-references a Student Charter, Regulations and Procedures for Students, and Fitness to Practice procedures as linked documents; the IEG policy does not reference these.
The UCP policy includes a mechanism for a student to take a temporary break and return to study; the IEG policy does not explicitly provide for a structured leave-of-absence pathway.
The UCP policy reserves the right to decide which procedure applies at any point in a case; the IEG policy does not include an equivalent discretionary override clause.
The UCP policy was approved by an Academic Standards and Quality Committee (ASQC); the IEG policy was approved by a Policy Approval Group, reflecting different governance structures.
contradictions: The IEG policy states it applies to all students with the explicit exception of UCP students, who are covered by a separate policy; the UCP policy confirms it applies exclusively to HE provision at UCP. These are consistent in intent but create a structural dependency — if the UCP policy is withdrawn or out of date, UCP students would have no coverage under the IEG policy.

The IEG policy lists failure to engage as potentially triggering disciplinary procedures or withdrawal as a relatively direct consequence; the UCP policy positions disciplinary procedures as a last resort to be avoided where possible, creating a different default disposition toward non-engaging students.
scope_differences: The IEG policy covers FE students and apprentices; the UCP policy explicitly excludes these groups.
The UCP policy covers sub-contractual HE provision; the IEG policy does not address sub-contractual arrangements.
The IEG policy addresses pre-admission and induction-period fitness concerns; the UCP policy does not cover pre-admission scenarios in the excerpted text.
The UCP policy explicitly includes behavioural, attitudinal, and social elements as wellbeing concerns within scope; the IEG policy focuses more narrowly on physical and mental health.
The UCP policy includes Fitness to Practice as a related procedure within its scope references; the IEG policy makes no reference to Fitness to Practice.

findings.ucp_policy: UCP-REC001 Higher Education Recruitment and Admissions Policy

comparison.summary: The IEG policy is a high-level FE admissions framework explicitly excluding HE, while the UCP policy is a detailed HE-specific admissions policy with formal regulatory and compliance requirements appropriate to a university centre.
substantive_differences: IEG policy explicitly excludes undergraduate/higher education courses from its scope; UCP policy applies exclusively to HE provision
UCP includes a formal Risk Analysis section addressing resource adequacy, reputational risk, and staff training obligations; IEG has no risk analysis section

UCP references a Student Protection Plan (SPP001) as a related document, a regulatory requirement for HE providers; IEG has no equivalent

UCP references a Recognition of Prior Learning/Experience Policy (APL001); IEG has no RPL provision referenced
UCP explicitly states international students requiring a student visa cannot apply or enrol; IEG makes no reference to international student visa restrictions

UCP oversight responsibility sits with the Academic Director; IEG oversight sits with the Vice Principal of Student and Staff Experience, reflecting different governance structures

UCP policy covers sub-contractual HE arrangements; IEG policy has no equivalent provision
IEG policy covers workplace and remote delivery modalities explicitly; UCP policy does not differentiate by delivery modality
UCP policy addresses international student visa eligibility (exclusion of visa-requiring students); IEG policy does not address international student visa status
UCP policy includes financial admissions terms (fees, refunds, terms and conditions); IEG policy does not address financial terms within its admissions scope
UCP policy includes Recognition of Prior Learning as an admissions consideration; IEG policy does not reference RPL
IEG policy references a Careers Education, Information, Advice and Guidance (CEIAG) policy as directly related; UCP has no CEIAG equivalent in its related documents

It is unclear whether these roles can each independently authorise up to £20,000, or whether any of them must act jointly. findings.observation: Section 3.1 states the policy applies 'exclusively to all higher education exams'. The word 'exclusively' restricts scope, yet 'all' broadens it without qualification. More importantly, the section then extends scope to 'sub contractual provision' without defining what that means or which awarding bodies or partners are covered. The tension between 'exclusively' and the open-ended subcontractual extension is likely to cause practitioners to misread the boundary of applicability.

findings.observation: Section 7.1 references 'the General Data Protection Regulation Data Protection Act, 2018'. This conflates two distinct instruments: the UK GDPR (retained EU law) and the Data Protection Act 2018. Post-Brexit, the operative domestic framework is the UK GDPR together with the DPA 2018, not the original EU GDPR. The reviewer should confirm the citation is accurate for the jurisdiction and period in which the policy operates, and whether any post-2021 UK-specific updates (e.g., changes proposed under the Data Protection and Digital Information Bill) affect compliance statements.

findings.observation: The confidentiality section states 'no further action will be taken without the student's permission unless the member of staff believes that significant health and safety, criminal, safeguarding or other risks are present.' However, the sexual violence paragraph immediately below mandates that such reports 'must be dealt with by the Safeguarding Team' without any reference to student permission. This creates a direct contradiction: a student reporting sexual harassment could reasonably believe they retain control over whether action is taken, when in practice they do not for this category of incident. The override condition should be explicitly stated upfront or the sexual violence paragraph should cross-reference the confidentiality caveat.
findings.location_hint: Reporting Bullying and Harassment section, paragraphs 1 and 3

findings.observation: 'The person investigating will... Where appropriate, bring both parties together.' For sexual violence and harassment cases, bringing victim and alleged perpetrator together is widely considered poor and potentially harmful practice. The policy does not exclude this category of incident from the 'bring both parties together' step, leaving practitioners to make an unsupported judgment call. A reviewer should check whether this omission creates a safeguarding risk and whether an explicit carve-out for sexual violence cases is needed.

findings.observation: 'Inform relevant members of the senior leadership team and parents/carers where this is deemed appropriate' is highly discretionary. For a sixth-form or further education college context (which this appears to be, given dual campuses and the mix of children and adults), notifying parents/carers may be legally appropriate for under-18s but a data protection and consent issue for adult students. The policy does not differentiate between these groups, leaving practitioners without clear guidance on when parental notification is permissible or required.

The phrase 'may be much the same or worse' is qualified and non-committal, and the rationale given (inability to delete) is only one of several aggravating factors for online bullying. A reviewer should check whether subcontractor employees are genuinely intended to be covered and whether this creates legal or practical ambiguity about who can invoke protected disclosure protections.

findings.observation: The policy states that 'a failure to raise a concern under this procedure may result in a disclosure losing its protected status under the law'. This is potentially misleading: under the Public Interest Disclosure Act / ERA 1996, protected status is determined by how and to whom a disclosure is made, not by whether an internal procedure was followed first. Workers may misread this as meaning they must always raise internally before going external, which could deter legitimate external disclosures to prescribed persons. The wording should be reviewed for accuracy and to avoid chilling effect.

findings.observation: 'Prior approval of the Board is required where the Net Book Value is over £25,000 and/or the sale is not open market.' The use of 'and/or' creates genuine ambiguity: does Board approval trigger when EITHER condition is met independently, or only when both are met together, or both? The logical consequence is significant — a low-value asset disposed of by donation (not open market) could be read as requiring Board approval under the 'or' reading. Practitioners commonly resolve 'and/or' differently; this should be rewritten as explicit separate conditions.

findings.observation: The policy references 'the United Kingdom Directive on Waste Electrical and Electronic Equipment (WEEE).' The original EU WEEE Directive was transposed into UK law via the WEEE Regulations 2013 (SI 2013/3113), which is the instrument that remained operative post-Brexit (as retained law). Describing it as a 'Directive' is technically inaccurate post-Brexit and may cause confusion about which legal instrument applies. The reviewer should confirm the reference has been updated to cite the correct domestic regulations, particularly given ongoing post-Brexit legislative updates.

findings.observation: Section 3.1 states the policy applies 'exclusively to all Higher Education provision' but also 'applies to sub contractual provisions.' These two statements may conflict depending on interpretation: 'exclusively' could be read as limiting scope, yet sub-contractual provision may involve delivery partners outside direct UCP control. The reviewer should check whether sub-contracted provision is governed by the same admissions process and whether a separate schedule or addendum is needed to address any differences.

findings.observation: Section 3.2 states that 'International students who require a student visa to study in the UK cannot apply or enrol to study courses at UCP.' This is an absolute prohibition but it is unclear whether it applies to all courses (including short courses, CPD, or online-only provision) or only UKVI-regulated courses. It is also ambiguous whether students who have existing right to remain or other visa status that does not require a Student visa route are covered. Practitioners may misread this as excluding all non-UK nationals. The reviewer should verify this reflects current UKVI licensing status and that the wording is precise enough to avoid unlawful discrimination against settled or pre-settled EU nationals or others with right to study.

findings.observation: Section 7.1 refers to 'the General Data Protection Regulation Data Protection Act, 2018.' The UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018 are distinct instruments post-Brexit. The phrasing conflates them and does not acknowledge that the relevant domestic framework is now the UK GDPR (retained and amended via the Data Protection, Privacy and Electronic Communications (Amendments etc) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019), not the EU GDPR. Depending on future legislative developments (the Data Protection and Digital Information Bill trajectory at the time of drafting), this reference may also need updating. The reviewer should ensure the citation accurately reflects current applicable UK law.

findings.observation: Section 8.3 sets two different response timeframes: 'within 10 working days' normally and 'may take 15 working days' during peak times. Neither 'peak application times' nor the trigger for switching between the two standards is defined. Practitioners cannot know which standard applies at any given time, and applicants cannot rely on a clear commitment. The reviewer should check whether these timeframes are consistent with any service standards committed to in the HE Student Charter or Terms and Conditions, and whether 'peak times' should be defined (e.g., by date range or application volume threshold).

The document then correctly cites 'www.ucas.com' for the cycle dates. An incorrect URL embedded in policy guidance could misdirect applicants or staff. This creates a potential barrier to access that may exclude eligible students who legitimately lack one of these three identifiers. However, the document does not clarify whether this age limit applies only to the initial year of eligibility or to all subsequent years of the same course. A student who is 24 when they start a three-year course would be 26 or 27 in later years — it is unclear whether the bursary continues throughout the course or is reassessed annually.

These two criteria conflict for top-up students, who are explicitly included in the 'new entrant' definition yet are not enrolled at UCP for the first time. findings.observation: The Scope section states that parents/carers of students aged under 18 'will be informed of disciplinary matters' and that 'once a student becomes 18 they may request that parents are not informed.' However, the Staff Responsibilities section states staff will maintain parent/carer contact 'up to the 31st August following their 18th Birthday.' These two provisions are not clearly reconciled: a student who turns 18 mid-year and requests non-contact could conflict with the staff obligation running to 31 August. The policy does not explain which provision takes precedence when a student aged 18 has actively requested non-contact but the August deadline has not yet passed.

findings.observation: The Scope section states parents/carers of students with an EHCP, high-needs funding, or other vulnerable adults 'will always be informed' of disciplinary action. The Staff Responsibilities section extends EHCP contact to age 25. There is no stated mechanism for what happens when a student aged 18–25 with an EHCP has requested that parents not be informed. The absolute 'always' in the Scope bullet appears to override the student's right to request non-contact, but this is never explicitly addressed or justified against data protection obligations.

This is ambiguous in a safeguarding context: practitioners may read this as discretionary when in some circumstances (e.g. immediate risk of harm) there may be a legal or safeguarding duty to act. The wording could lead to inconsistent responses to serious risk situations. A clearer threshold or cross-reference to the Safeguarding Policy is needed.

The word 'solely' is ambiguous: it is unclear whether this means students enrolled only at the college (not dual-registered at a school) or students who are physically on college premises at the time of the incident. findings.observation: Section 6.1 refers to 'the General Data Protection Regulation Data Protection Act, 2018' — this conflates two separate instruments (UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018). More importantly, the UK GDPR (retained post-Brexit) is now the operative instrument in domestic law, not the original EU GDPR. The drafting should be checked to confirm it references the correct post-Brexit legislation and has not simply copied pre-2021 boilerplate. The reviewer should also verify whether UCP's DPA/UK GDPR compliance statement is handled by a dedicated Data Protection Policy that should be cross-referenced rather than summarised here.

findings.observation: Section 7.2 grants UCP a 'royalty-free, irrevocable and non-exclusive licence' over student IP. The term 'irrevocable' is legally significant but is never explained to students in plain language, despite section 1.1 instructing all students to read the policy carefully to understand its implications. A student could reasonably misread this as a limited administrative licence rather than a permanent, unconditional right that survives graduation or withdrawal. The policy should either define 'irrevocable' in context or signpost students to seek independent advice.

findings.observation: Section 7.3 states that students 'may need to assign ownership' of IP in externally sponsored work, and that 'the need for an assignment will be determined by UCP'. No criteria, timeline, process, or right of appeal are specified for this determination. Practitioners (HE Managers, academic staff) have no basis for consistent decision-making, and students have no basis for challenging a determination. This is a significant gap for a governance-level document.

findings.observation: The dispute resolution clause states the Group will 'submit to independent outside arbitration or mediation and abide by its findings.' Arbitration and mediation are materially different processes — arbitration produces a binding determination, whereas mediation typically does not. Committing to 'abide by' mediation findings may be legally unenforceable and create confusion about whether the outcome is binding. A reviewer should check whether this distinction is intentional and whether contract templates reflect it accurately.

findings.observation: Section 6.1 refers to 'the General Data Protection Regulation Data Protection Act, 2018'. The UK GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018 are two distinct instruments post-Brexit, and the phrasing conflates them. More importantly, following the UK's departure from the EU, the operative instrument is the UK GDPR (retained in domestic law), not the EU GDPR. The reference should be reviewed to ensure it accurately reflects the current UK legal framework, and governance reviewers should check whether the organisation's standard data protection clause template has been updated accordingly.

findings.observation: Section 2.3 describes Stage 3 as allowing the student to appeal 'to a higher level within UCP or the awarding body (e.g. OU, Pearson, BGU)'. The word 'or' is ambiguous: it is unclear whether the student has a free choice between UCP and the awarding body, whether it depends on circumstances, or whether UCP is always the first port of call before escalating to the awarding body. Practitioners could reasonably read this in at least two different ways, potentially directing students to the wrong body.

Referencing 'the General Data Protection Regulation' without qualification may be technically inaccurate for a post-2020 document and should be reviewed to ensure it cites current applicable UK legislation.
