findings.observation: The policy header states it was 'Approved: Academic Board 10/10/25' with a review date of '31 July 2026' — a review window of approximately nine months. However, this is Issue 4, suggesting an established policy. A reviewer should confirm whether the short review cycle is intentional (e.g. triggered by regulatory change) or whether the review date was set in error, and whether the approval date of 10 October 2025 is a future date at time of drafting, which would mean the policy was not yet in force when circulated.

findings.observation: The policy states under 'Software & Other Intangibles' that 'The useful lives of Goodwill and Intangible are presumed to have an infinite life under FRS102. This can either be estimated based on reliable evidence, or restricted to 10 years.' This is internally contradictory: FRS 102 (Section 18) does not presume infinite useful life for intangibles — it states that where useful life cannot be reliably estimated, it should be presumed not to exceed 10 years. The phrase 'infinite life' misrepresents the standard and conflicts with the 10-year cap mentioned in the same sentence. Reviewer should check whether this is a drafting error or a misapplication of FRS 102.
findings.location_hint: Appendix 1, 'Software & Other Intangibles' section, paragraph beginning 'The useful lives of Goodwill and Intangible...'
findings.policy_name: FIN008 – Fixed Asset Capitalisation and Depreciation Policy
findings.disclaimer: AI-generated prompt for governance review. Not a verified finding.

findings.observation: The capitalisation threshold table shows two thresholds in force: £2,000 from 1st August 2023 and £5,000 from 1st August 2024. The policy was issued in July 2024 and the higher threshold takes effect one month after issue. It is unclear whether the body of the policy (and any asset register decisions made between issue and August 2024) should apply the old or new threshold. There is no transitional guidance explaining how assets valued between £2,000 and £5,000 acquired before 1st August 2024 should be treated going forward (e.g. whether they should be retained on the register or written off).
findings.location_hint: Appendix 1, 'Capitalisation Thresholds' table
findings.policy_name: FIN008 – Fixed Asset Capitalisation and Depreciation Policy

findings.observation: The 'Exclusions from the Asset Register' section states peripheral equipment 'bought in bulk, funded by grant funding will be expensed, while being classed as part of a capital project, subject to the application of the limits detailed above.' The phrase 'subject to the application of the limits detailed above' is ambiguous: it could mean (a) items are only excluded if they fall below the capitalisation threshold, or (b) the exclusion applies regardless of value. This is a commonly misread clause — practitioners may be uncertain whether a bulk grant-funded purchase of microscopes above £5,000 in total should be capitalised or expensed.
findings.location_hint: Appendix 1, 'Exclusions from the Asset Register' section

findings.observation: The capitalisation threshold table heading reads 'Single/Multiple Item Threshold' but the policy body does not define how 'multiple item' aggregation works in practice. It is unclear whether items must be of the same type, purchased in the same transaction, or delivered to the same location to be aggregated. This is a well-known area of practitioner confusion and the absence of an aggregation rule creates a risk of inconsistent treatment across the group.

findings.observation: The internal staff costs section states costs 'will be capitalised if the member of staff's time can be directly attributed to one or more capital projects' and that these will 'usually be in the Estates or IT functions.' The word 'usually' implies staff in other functions could also capitalise time, but no further criteria or approval process is given for exceptions outside those functions. This creates a risk of inconsistent or unsupported capitalisation of staff costs across the group.

findings.observation: The policy references 'Managing Public Money' as a compliance framework. Reviewer should verify that the version of Managing Public Money being applied is current (HM Treasury updates it periodically) and that it is an appropriate and directly applicable framework for an academy trust/education group context, as its primary audience is central government departments. If IEG is an academy trust, the relevant framework is the Academy Trust Handbook (ESFA), which has more specific fixed asset requirements. findings.observation: The document repeatedly references 'General Data Protection Regulations May 2018' and 'General Data Protection Regulation 2018' as the governing legislation. In the UK post-Brexit context (applicable from 1 January 2021), the operative law is the UK GDPR (retained under the Data Protection Act 2018), not the EU GDPR. The document does not acknowledge UK GDPR or the Data Protection Act 2018 at all. A reviewer should confirm whether IEG operates solely in the UK and, if so, whether the policy correctly names the applicable legislation.

findings.observation: The Responsibilities section states that the DPO 'is responsible for all day-to-day data protection matters' and for ensuring staff abide by the policy, but in the very next sentence it states that 'the Inspire Education Chief Financial Officer will manage GDPR compliance for the Group.' These two statements assign overlapping but undefined authority to two different role-holders. It is unclear whether the CFO has supervisory authority over the DPO, whether their remits are divided, or which role takes precedence in a conflict. This also raises a potential regulatory concern: under UK GDPR Article 38, the DPO must not receive instructions regarding the exercise of their tasks, and subordinating the DPO's compliance function to the CFO could be incompatible with that requirement.

findings.observation: The Scope section states that third-party agencies and individuals 'will be expected to read and comply with this policy and complete mandatory GDPR training' but frames this as 'a matter of best practice' rather than a contractual or enforceable requirement. Immediately afterwards the policy states that departments must ensure external bodies 'sign a contract which includes an agreement to confirm that they have read and will abide by this policy.' Practitioners may misread 'best practice' as optional, creating inconsistency in how third-party obligations are actually enforced. The weaker framing could also be insufficient to satisfy UK GDPR Article 28 processor contract requirements.

If that policy changes or is renamed, the cross-reference becomes untraceable. A reviewer should confirm the referenced policy exists, is current, and that its identifier or location is cited.

findings.observation: The document mentions invoking 'the Group's disciplinary procedures' for breaches but does not name, link, or cross-reference a Disciplinary Policy. Practitioners need to know where to find that procedure. A reviewer should confirm the disciplinary policy exists, is current, and is referenced by name or document ID.

findings.observation: The Policy Statement commits to undertaking 'Data Protection Impact Assessments' (DPIAs) where high risk to rights and freedoms exists, but there is no reference to a DPIA procedure, template, or guidance document. Without this, practitioners have no direction on how or when to trigger a DPIA. A reviewer should verify whether a separate DPIA procedure exists and ensure it is cross-referenced here.

findings.observation: The ICO notification link embedded in the Responsibilities section appears to be truncated ('https://www.gov.uk/notification-to-proce...') and is therefore non-functional as printed. Additionally, the correct ICO website is ico.org.uk, not gov.uk; the URL as written would not reach the ICO. A reviewer should verify and correct both the domain and the complete URL.

findings.disclaimer: AI-generated prompt for governance review. Not a verified finding.

findings.observation: The document lists 'criminal records' under the definition of personal data alongside Special Categories, but under UK GDPR criminal records data (Article 10) is a distinct category with its own separate legal basis requirements — it is not technically a 'Special Category' under Article 9. Conflating the two could lead practitioners to apply the wrong lawful basis conditions. A reviewer should check whether this distinction is adequately captured or addressed elsewhere.

findings.disclaimer: AI-generated prompt for governance review. Not a verified finding.

findings.observation: The National Careers Strategy cited was published in December 2017. A refreshed careers strategy was published by the UK Government in 2022 ('Careers Strategy: Refreshed'). The reviewer should confirm whether the 2017 document remains the operative reference or whether this policy should cite the more current guidance, as using an outdated strategy reference may undermine alignment claims and Ofsted scrutiny.

findings.observation: The reference to 'Local Enterprise Partnerships' (LEPs) may be outdated. The UK Government announced in 2023 that LEP functions would be absorbed into combined authorities and local councils, with many LEPs winding down by March 2024. Given the policy was issued November 2024, this reference may no longer reflect the correct governance structures. The reviewer should check whether the relevant body is now a Mayoral Combined Authority, a county-level body, or another successor organisation.

findings.observation: The Purpose section states the programme is designed to 'inspire, inform, advice and guide' — 'advice' should read 'advise' (verb form). While likely a typographical error, in a policy document this could create ambiguity about whether the word is intended as a noun (a discrete advice function) or a verb, potentially affecting how practitioners interpret the scope of their delivery obligations.

findings.observation: The Scope section excludes provision delivered by University Centre Peterborough (UCP) on the basis that 'a separate policy is in place', but does not name or reference that policy. Practitioners delivering blended or cross-provision programmes involving both IEG and UCP students may be unclear which policy governs their CEIAG obligations in those contexts. The separate UCP policy should be named and cross-referenced.

findings.observation: The Responsibilities section states 'All Student Mentors across the Group are to ensure careers advice and guidance is available and promoted', which implies an active assurance duty. However, the policy elsewhere positions impartial advice as the specific responsibility of the IAG teams and careers advisors. Placing an 'ensure' obligation on Student Mentors — who are unlikely to be qualified careers advisers — creates a potential conflict with the impartiality and competence requirements embedded in the Gatsby Benchmarks (particularly Benchmark 8 on personal guidance). The reviewer should check whether 'promote awareness of' was the intended obligation rather than 'ensure availability of'.

This wording could be misread by practitioners as limiting the careers advisor's remit to under-19s, leaving adult learners without a clearly assigned responsible party for personalised guidance.

findings.observation: The Scope section states students have a right to appeal if they 'Disagree with a centre's decision to reject internally assessed work on the grounds of malpractice by the student.' This directly conflicts with the Purpose statement's commitment to protecting 'the integrity of qualifications' and the broader policy framework around malpractice. Permitting a student to appeal a malpractice rejection through the same internal academic appeals channel conflates two distinct processes (academic appeals vs. malpractice appeals), which typically have separate procedures under JCQ regulations. Reviewer should check whether this ground for appeal is intentional and whether it should instead signpost to a separate malpractice/misconduct policy.

findings.policy_name: Academic Appeals Policy
findings.disclaimer: AI-generated prompt for governance review. Not a verified finding.

findings.observation: The Scope section bullet point is grammatically incomplete and internally inconsistent: it begins 'Believe that a piece of work submitted for assessment has been assessed unfairly...' and then mid-sentence restates 'shall have the right to appeal against the assessment mark, grade or final outcome' — duplicating the framing from the introductory sentence. This creates a structural loop that could confuse practitioners about whether the right to appeal is conditional on the criteria listed or unconditional as stated in Purpose ('Any Student dissatisfied with a grade has the right to appeal').

findings.observation: The policy states a student at an appeal hearing 'will have the right to be accompanied by another person (who may be a student of the College, a relative or an employer representative)' but does not state whether a trade union representative, friend, or legal advisor is permitted or excluded. 'May be' is permissive but the list could be read as exhaustive, which would be restrictive and potentially inconsistent with natural justice principles. Reviewer should confirm whether the list is intended to be illustrative or exhaustive and whether legal representation should be explicitly addressed.

findings.observation: The Teacher responsibilities section states documents must be made available 'within 3 working days' but does not specify the trigger point for this deadline (e.g., 3 working days from when — from the student's request, from marks being released, or from a formal appeal being lodged). This is routinely misread by practitioners, with some treating it as 3 days from submission of marks and others from receipt of a student request. The ambiguity creates compliance risk against JCQ requirements.

findings.observation: The policy assigns the 'final decision if there is any disagreement on the mark to be submitted to the Awarding Body' to the Head of the Centre, but earlier assigns primary responsibility for the review process to the Assistant Principal Quality. It is unclear what threshold constitutes 'disagreement,' who the parties to that disagreement are (reviewer vs. original teacher? AP Quality vs. Head of Centre?), and whether the student is notified before the Head of Centre makes this final internal decision. This could undermine the student's right to be 'kept informed at all stages.'

findings.observation: The Head of Faculty is listed as responsible for 'Clearly communicating progression decisions to parents/carers in a timely manner.' The policy does not define 'timely,' nor does it address GDPR/data sharing considerations for students who are adults (18+), where communication to parents/carers without consent may be unlawful. This is a commonly overlooked issue in FE/HE contexts. Reviewer should check whether a data sharing or parental engagement policy is cross-referenced.

findings.observation: The policy references the 'Joint Council for Qualifications (JCQ)' regulations as a compliance framework but does not cite a specific JCQ document, version, or year. JCQ updates its procedures annually (e.g., 'Post-Results Services' and 'General Regulations for Approved Centres' are revised each academic year). Without a versioned reference, there is a risk the policy is operating against superseded JCQ guidance. Reviewer should verify that the procedural steps described (particularly the internal review before Awarding Body appeal) remain consistent with the current JCQ cycle.

In a post-2018 context, an appeals policy that handles personal data (marked work, written outcomes, malpractice allegations) should cross-reference the institution's data protection framework or at minimum acknowledge retention and access obligations. Absence of this reference is a governance gap.

findings.observation: The issue date in the header reads '19/12/2025' but the approval date recorded is '29-09-2024'. If the document was approved in September 2024, an issue date of December 2025 is anomalous — it post-dates the stated review date logic and raises the question of whether this is a future-dated version or a data entry error. The review date of '26 December 2026' is also unusually specific (a public holiday) and may indicate a copy-paste or calculation error.

findings.observation: Section 3.7 states the original fee regime applies for 'the normal course length plus two years', after which students are moved to the new regime — but it then immediately carves out an exception for students 'eligible for tuition fee loans under an old fee regime'. The phrase 'old fee regime' is undefined and circular (any prior regime could qualify). Practitioners may apply this exception inconsistently, potentially affecting student liability calculations.

findings.observation: Section 3.9 states fees 'may increase annually subject to setting by central government' and then in the same bullet cluster states 'we intend to increase fees each year using the RPI-X'. These two statements are potentially contradictory: the first conditions increases on government decisions, while the second implies a standing intention to increase regardless. 'RPI-X' is also unexplained — there is no definition of what the X deduction is, who sets it, or where it is published, leaving practitioners unable to calculate or communicate increases accurately.

findings.observation: Section 3.6 states part-time fees at Stamford and Peterborough are charged 'pro rata of 100% of the full-time course fees' based on the standard study route, but then states franchise part-time fees are 'set annual amounts'. It is unclear whether the pro-rata rule applies to UCP-delivered part-time courses only, or also to some franchise arrangements. A reviewer should check whether the intended distinction is unambiguous to admissions and finance staff, and whether franchise contracts are consistent with this statement.

findings.observation: Section 4.2 references the 'Unfair Terms in Consumer Contracts Regulations 1999 (UTCCRs)' and acknowledges this is the legislation 'at the date of publication'. However, UTCCRs were largely superseded by the Consumer Rights Act 2015, which consolidated and replaced unfair terms provisions for B2C contracts. The parenthetical caveat does not fully protect the document — a reviewer should verify whether the CRA 2015 should now be the primary citation, and whether reliance on UTCCRs creates compliance risk.

More substantively, the Digital Markets, Competition and Consumers Act 2024 amends the consumer protection enforcement landscape; a reviewer should confirm whether this citation remains sufficient or requires updating to reflect current regulatory context.

findings.observation: The policy states IEG will take 'all reasonable steps' to protect staff from third-party harassment under the Worker Protection Act 2023, but provides no definition of what constitutes reasonable steps in the IEG context. The Worker Protection Act 2023 uses 'reasonable steps' as a legal threshold for the preventative duty, and without operational detail this creates compliance risk — regulators and employment tribunals will look for evidence of what those steps are. Practitioners cannot self-assess compliance against this standard.

findings.observation: The policy references 'preparing for the evolving legislative landscape of the Employment Rights Bill.' The Employment Rights Bill received Royal Assent in July 2025, becoming the Employment Rights Act 2025. Given the policy issue date is March 2026, this reference should have been updated from a prospective Bill to the enacted Act with its applicable commencement dates. Retaining 'Bill' language post-enactment is factually incorrect and may undermine the policy's credibility in any formal review or legal context.

If the policy was drafted against an earlier edition, any procedural obligations derived from it may be out of date. The reviewer should verify which edition was in scope when the policy was approved.

findings.observation: 'Best endeavours' is used in both the Purpose section and the Policy Statement bullet points. In SEND law, 'best endeavours' has a specific statutory meaning (Children and Families Act 2014, s.66) that is stronger than general effort but weaker than an absolute duty. Using it alongside language like 'ensuring' (which implies an absolute obligation) in adjacent bullet points creates inconsistency about the actual duty level. Practitioners may misread 'ensuring' bullets as absolute guarantees, or conversely water down genuine duties by assuming best endeavours applies throughout. The reviewer should check whether each commitment is accurately characterised as a duty, a best endeavours obligation, or an aspiration.

findings.observation: The title references 'Open University Provision' but the policy code and originator reference 'UCP' (University Centre Peterborough or similar). It is unclear whether this handbook governs UCP-delivered Open University modules, UCP's own open/distance provision, or a partnership arrangement. The scope section (if present beyond the truncated text) should explicitly define which students, programmes and delivery modes are in scope. Practitioners may apply it inconsistently.

findings.observation: Section 4.11 is titled 'Assessment Qualify Marks' — this is likely a typographical corruption of 'Qualifying Marks' (pass/fail thresholds that must be met independently of the aggregate). The term as written is not standard and practitioners may not understand what threshold rule is being applied or whether it differs from compensatable fails. The actual content is not visible in the extract, increasing risk of misapplication.

findings.observation: Section 4.12 uses the hybrid term 'In class-tests/ Internally invigilated tests' without defining the distinction between the two, or clarifying whether these fall within or outside the scope of a handbook explicitly titled 'Non-examination Assessment'. If internally invigilated tests are considered examinations under the partner university's regulations, including them here without cross-referencing an examination policy creates a governance gap and potential contradiction with any separate examination regulations.

findings.observation: The handbook is explicitly scoped to 'Non-examination Assessment' in its title, yet Section 8 is titled 'Examination Boards'. While 'Examination Board' is a standard UK HE term for the award/progression decision-making body (regardless of assessment mode), the inconsistency between the title's exclusion of 'examination' and the prominent use of 'Examination Boards' as a section heading may cause confusion about scope. A reviewer should check whether the partner university's own terminology is 'Assessment Board' or 'Progression and Award Board' and whether alignment is required.

findings.observation: Section 7 ('Academic Misconduct') is listed as a standalone section but appears, from the Table of Contents, to contain no subsections and likely only a short entry. There is no visible cross-reference to a dedicated Academic Misconduct / Academic Integrity policy, nor to the partner university's (Open University's) own academic integrity regulations, which would normally take precedence in a franchise/partnership arrangement. This is a significant governance gap: practitioners may not know which institution's procedure applies, who investigates, and at which stage cases are referred upward.

findings.observation: Section 4.8 ('Extensions and Extenuating Circumstances') is listed without visible subsections or cross-reference to a dedicated Extenuating Circumstances policy. In a partnership context it is essential to specify whether UCP's own EC procedure or the Open University's EC procedure governs, and whether decisions made under one are binding on the other's Examination Board. Absence of this cross-reference is a material omission.

A reviewer should confirm (a) that the approval date reflects actual committee approval rather than a placeholder, and (b) whether a two-year review cycle is consistent with the partner university's requirements and with the frequency of regulatory change (e.g. OfS ongoing condition of registration updates, changes to QAA Quality Code). findings.observation: Section 4.2 ('Anonymous Marking') spans from page 7 to page 9 — a notably large allocation suggesting substantive content — while Section 4.3 ('Marking grid') spans pages 9 to 20, covering eleven pages. A reviewer should verify that the anonymous marking provisions are consistent with the marking grid criteria (e.g. that anonymity is maintained through all stages of the grid-referenced marking process) and that any exceptions to anonymous marking (e.g. portfolio, practical, presentation) are explicitly listed and do not contradict the general requirement.

findings.observation: Section 4.7 ('Late submission') and Section 4.8 ('Extensions and Extenuating Circumstances') are listed as separate sections but their interaction is not defined in the visible extract. A common practitioner misreading is to apply a late penalty and grant an extension simultaneously, or to process an EC claim as a standard extension. The policy should (and may not) define a clear decision hierarchy between these provisions. This is especially important in a partnership context where the two institutions may have different default penalties.

findings.observation: The Purpose section states the policy protects 'all FE students' and uses 'child-on-child abuse' terminology, yet FE colleges routinely enrol students aged 18 and over. The Definitions section partially addresses this by distinguishing 'child-on-child abuse (for students under 18)' from 'peer-on-peer harassment', but the Purpose section does not make this distinction, potentially implying child-safeguarding framing applies universally. A reviewer should check whether the boundary between the two frameworks is applied consistently throughout all sections, including the appendices and flowcharts.

findings.observation: The Responsibilities section states staff must record 'low-level concerns' on Pro Monitor, but does not define what system Pro Monitor is, who has access to it, or what the escalation threshold is from a recorded low-level concern to active safeguarding intervention. findings.observation: The lawful basis section conflates 'contract' and 'legitimate interests' without clearly mapping individual processing activities to their specific lawful basis. For special category data (health/disability, ethnic origin, sexual orientation, religion, criminal records, trade union membership), UK GDPR Article 9 requires an explicit additional condition beyond Article 6, and Schedule 1 of the Data Protection Act 2018 requires a documented policy document for most Schedule 1 conditions. The notice does not identify which Article 9 condition or Schedule 1 condition applies to each category of sensitive data, which is a substantive compliance gap rather than merely a drafting issue.
